name: Department of Letters EULA
classification_notes: The page contains no typeface specimen or font product information; it is a legal licence agreement only. No classification is possible from the available content.
summary: This page is a licence agreement for fonts sold by Carl Shura at Department of Letters, a Canadian type foundry based in Ontario.
description: The page presents the End User License Agreement governing all font purchases from Department of Letters, operated by Carl Shura. It covers permitted uses, restrictions on embedding, modification, transfer and commercial letterform products, along with terms on intellectual property ownership and dispute resolution under Ontario and Canadian law. No specific typeface is described or shown on this page.
foundry_blurb: Department of Letters, operated by Carl Shura, provides a perpetual, non-exclusive worldwide licence for its fonts upon purchase. The agreement sets out permitted uses including backup copying and printer transmission, while restricting embedding, redistribution, and use in letterform reproduction products. Disputes are governed by the laws of the Province of Ontario and Canadian federal law.

name: Circulation Serif
classification_notes: Inspired by classical Dutch text typefaces and humanist mid-century design, with calligraphic details and high x-height typical of the humanist serif tradition.
summary: A single-weight humanist serif drawing on Dutch classical text faces, designed for long-form reading at small sizes with strong editorial range at display scales.
description: Built around open apertures, a high x-height and calligraphic detail inherited from the Dutch text tradition, it performs reliably at small sizes from 6pt to 12pt and scales comfortably into headline use. The glyph set of 481 characters covers 200+ Latin-script languages and includes tabular oldstyle figures, fractions, discretionary ligatures and case-responsive diacritics. It received a Communication Arts Award of Excellence in Type Design in 2022.
foundry_blurb: Department of Letters describe Circulation Serif as intended for long-form text at small sizes while remaining versatile for headlines and display. They draw on classical Dutch typefaces and humanist mid-century design to produce what they call a rational but sensitive voice, suited to editorial, academic and communications contexts where clarity and trust are required.

name: Circulation Sans
classification_notes: The typeface draws from classical Dutch text faces and humanist mid-century design, with calligraphic detail, open apertures and a high x-height — hallmarks of the humanist sans tradition.
summary: A humanist sans rooted in Dutch text face traditions, optimised for long-form reading at small sizes with open apertures and a high x-height.
description: Drawing on classical Dutch typefaces and humanist mid-century design, it brings calligraphic detail to a rational, sober structure suited to editorial, academic and communications work. Open apertures and a generous x-height keep it legible at 6pt to 12pt, while the forms hold up well in headlines and display settings. The single-weight release covers 480 glyphs with support for over 200 Latin-script languages and a full suite of OpenType features including tabular old-style figures, fractions, contextual ligatures and case-responsive diacritics.
foundry_blurb: Department of Letters describe Circulation Sans as a rational but sensitive voice built for clear communications, conversations and new ideas. Inspired by the calligraphic hand and the Dutch tradition of text faces, it is intended for long-form text at small sizes while remaining versatile at display scale, with primary use cases in editorial, academic and communications contexts.

name: Mullion
classification_notes: A single ultra-heavy weight sans-serif drawing on late 19th and early 20th century grotesque models, with dominant vertical strokes and strong counterspaces; its weight and display intent place it firmly in the display category rather than a general-use grotesque.
summary: A single ultra-heavy grotesque drawing on late 19th and early 20th century advertising and signpainting traditions, built around strong vertical strokes and tight counterspaces.
description: Conceived around a dominant vertical axis, Mullion takes its name from the architectural term for the vertical dividing element in a window. That vertical emphasis is channelled into the letterform counterspaces, resulting in a face of considerable weight suited to editorial headers, identities and logomarks. Currently released as a single Heavy weight with 443 glyphs, three numeral styles and support for 200+ Latin-script languages, with a broader family of weights and styles planned.
foundry_blurb: Department of Letters describe Mullion as an ultra-heavy sans inspired by late 19th and early 20th century grotesques, with influences from advertising, commercial art and signpainters' style guides. The name comes from the architectural term for a vertical dividing element, reflecting the typeface's strong vertical vocabulary. It was recognised with a CA Award of Excellence in Type Design at the Communication Arts Typography Awards 2025.
